description: Ad copy evaluation rubric. 10 dimensions, weighted. Max weighted score: 66.25. Thresholds: Production-ready >= 53 | Strong draft 46-52 | Needs work 33-45 | Rewrite < 33.

dimensions.name: Angle Clarity
dimensions.description: Is the strategic angle unmistakable in one sentence?

scoring_guide.1: No clear angle. Could be about anything. Reader cannot identify the strategic position.
scoring_guide.2: Angle is vaguely present but buried or muddled with other themes.
scoring_guide.3: Angle is present but shares space with competing themes. Reader needs effort to identify the core message.
scoring_guide.4: Angle is clear and dominant. Minor distractions exist but don't confuse the primary message.
scoring_guide.5: Angle is unmistakable. Every sentence reinforces a single strategic position. A viewer could state the angle in one sentence.

dimensions.name: Motivation Match
dimensions.description: Does the ad hit the intended buying motivation — the felt need, not just the stated need?

scoring_guide.1: Motivation is absent or wrong. The ad speaks to a need the audience does not feel.
scoring_guide.2: Motivation is present but superficial. It names the category without tapping the emotional driver.
scoring_guide.3: Motivation is addressed but at a rational level only. The felt need is implied, not activated.
scoring_guide.4: Motivation is clearly present and emotionally resonant. The reader sees themselves in the copy.
scoring_guide.5: Motivation is deeply felt. The ad articulates what the audience feels but hasn't put into words. Immediate recognition.

dimensions.name: Tactic Execution
dimensions.description: Is the chosen tactic (PAS, BAB, cost-of-inaction, etc.) properly structured? Hook flows to body flows to CTA?

scoring_guide.1: No recognisable tactic structure. Copy is a list of features with no narrative logic.
scoring_guide.2: Tactic is attempted but breaks down. Hook doesn't connect to body, or body doesn't lead to CTA.
scoring_guide.3: Tactic is present and mostly follows the framework, but one transition is weak or forced.
scoring_guide.4: Tactic is well-executed. Hook, body, and CTA form a coherent arc with smooth transitions.
scoring_guide.5: Tactic is flawlessly executed. Each section builds on the last. The structure itself is invisible — it just reads naturally.

dimensions.name: Specificity
dimensions.description: Concrete numbers, names, and details — not vague claims. Every number must trace to verified facts.

scoring_guide.1: Entirely vague. No numbers, no concrete details. 'Save money on vet care.'
scoring_guide.2: One concrete detail present but surrounded by vague claims. Numbers may be unverifiable.
scoring_guide.3: Multiple concrete details present. Some claims remain vague. Most numbers are fact-checked.
scoring_guide.4: Consistently specific throughout. Numbers are verifiable. Only minor generalizations remain.
scoring_guide.5: Every claim is concrete and fact-checked. Specific costs, counts, timeframes, and outcomes. Zero vague filler.

dimensions.name: Objection Pre-emption
dimensions.description: Does the ad anticipate and address the top blocker for this angle before the reader raises it?

scoring_guide.1: No objection addressed. The most obvious 'but...' is left unanswered.
scoring_guide.2: Objection is weakly hinted at but not directly addressed. Reader still has the concern.
scoring_guide.3: Primary objection is addressed but the counter-argument is vague or unsupported.
scoring_guide.4: Primary objection is clearly pre-empted with specific proof. One secondary objection remains.
scoring_guide.5: Top objection is pre-empted convincingly with proof, and a secondary objection is also addressed. Reader feels like their concerns were read.

dimensions.name: Receptionist Test
dimensions.description: Could a clinic receptionist answer 'What is this, what's included, what does it cost, how much do I save, and how do I start?' from the ad alone?

scoring_guide.1: Fails the test. Missing 3+ of the 5 questions (what, included, cost, savings, how to start).
scoring_guide.2: Answers 2 of 5 clearly. Major gaps remain — receptionist would need to look things up.
scoring_guide.3: Answers 3 of 5 clearly. One or two gaps but the core proposition is understandable.
scoring_guide.4: Answers 4 of 5 clearly. Only one minor gap. Receptionist could confidently explain the offer.
scoring_guide.5: All 5 questions answered. What it is, what's included, what it costs, how much you save, and how to start are all explicit.

dimensions.name: Scroll-Stop Hook
dimensions.description: Would line 1 of the primary text stop a thumb-scrolling pet owner in their feed?

scoring_guide.1: Generic or boring opener. 'Looking for a better way to care for your pet?' No reason to stop.
scoring_guide.2: Mildly interesting but not arresting. Could be any brand or product.
scoring_guide.3: Hook is relevant and somewhat engaging. Would slow a scroll but not necessarily stop it.
scoring_guide.4: Strong hook that creates curiosity, recognition, or surprise. Most target readers would pause.
scoring_guide.5: Exceptional hook. Immediately personal, specific, and impossible to ignore for the target audience. Thumb stops.

dimensions.name: CTA Clarity
dimensions.description: Is the next step obvious, low-friction, and matched to the funnel stage?

scoring_guide.1: No CTA, or CTA is confusing ('Learn more about our wellness solutions').
scoring_guide.2: CTA exists but is vague or mismatched to funnel stage (BOF ad with TOF CTA).
scoring_guide.3: CTA is clear but generic. Reader knows what to do but isn't compelled.
scoring_guide.4: CTA is clear, low-friction, and matches the funnel stage. Minor wording improvements possible.
scoring_guide.5: CTA is crystal clear, frictionless, and perfectly funnel-matched. Reader knows exactly what happens next and wants to do it.

dimensions.name: Platform Fit
dimensions.description: Is the copy conversational, formatted for feed consumption, and the right length for the placement?

scoring_guide.1: Reads like a brochure, email, or landing page. Wrong tone and structure for social.
scoring_guide.2: Partially conversational but too long, too dense, or uses format-specific language.
scoring_guide.3: Conversational tone, reasonable length, but formatting could be tighter (wall of text, no line breaks).
scoring_guide.4: Good platform fit. Conversational, well-formatted, scannable. Works across feed placements.
scoring_guide.5: Native to the platform. Reads like a friend's post, not an ad. Perfect length, natural line breaks, scannable structure.

dimensions.name: Differentiation
dimensions.description: Is this ad distinct from the other ads in the same set? Does it offer a unique perspective?

scoring_guide.1: Duplicate of another ad in the set. Same hook, same structure, same message.
scoring_guide.2: Surface-level differences only (different opening word, same argument and flow).
scoring_guide.3: Different hook or structure but the core argument overlaps with another ad in the set.
scoring_guide.4: Clearly distinct angle or execution. A reader seeing both ads would recognize different messages.
scoring_guide.5: Completely unique perspective within the set. Different emotional entry point, different proof, different structure. Adds real creative diversity.

dimensions.name: Opening Diversity
dimensions.description: Does this ad open differently from other ads of the same content type? Measures first-word and first-three-word uniqueness across the ad set.

scoring_guide.1: First 3 words identical to another same-type ad. Feels like a copy-paste opening.
scoring_guide.2: First word shared with 3+ other same-type ads. The set sounds monotone at the start.
scoring_guide.3: First word shared with 2 other same-type ads. Some opening variety but room to diversify.
scoring_guide.4: First word shared with only 1 other same-type ad. Good opening variety.
scoring_guide.5: First 3 words are unique across all same-type ads. Every ad opens with a distinct voice.

dimensions.name: Sentence Variance
dimensions.description: Does the copy have rhythmic variety — a mix of short punchy sentences and longer narrative ones? Measures standard deviation of sentence word counts.

scoring_guide.1: Monotone — all sentences are the same length (std dev <= 1). Reads like a list.
scoring_guide.2: Minimal variety (std dev > 1). Slight length differences but still flat rhythm.
scoring_guide.3: Moderate variety (std dev > 3). Some short and long sentences but could be more dynamic.
scoring_guide.4: Good rhythm (std dev > 5). Mix of punchy and expansive sentences creates flow.
scoring_guide.5: Great rhythm variety (std dev > 8). Copy has a natural, engaging cadence with deliberate pacing.

dimensions.name: Emotional Register
dimensions.description: Does this ad use a different emotional opening style (question, story, statistic, contrast, direct, confession) from the rest of the ad set? Measures register diversity.

scoring_guide.1: Over 50% of same-type ads share this register. The set is locked into one opening style.
scoring_guide.2: 30-50% of same-type ads share this register. Common approach, limited diversity.
scoring_guide.3: 15-30% of same-type ads share this register. Moderate diversity.
scoring_guide.4: Less than 15% of same-type ads share this register. Good variety in opening styles.
scoring_guide.5: Unique register — no other same-type ad opens this way. Adds real creative diversity to the set.

dimensions.name: LP Readability
dimensions.description: Landing page readability — scannability, paragraph density, sentence length, formatting quality.

scoring_guide.1: Wall of text with no visual breaks. Long sentences, em dashes, bloated subhead. Unreadable.
scoring_guide.2: Minimal breaks, dense paragraphs, average sentence length over 25 words. Hard to scan.
scoring_guide.3: Some paragraph structure but still dense. Sentence length moderate. Minor formatting issues.
scoring_guide.4: Good scannability with clear paragraphs, concise sentences (10-20 words avg), concise subhead. Minor issues.
scoring_guide.5: Excellent readability. 6+ short paragraphs, 10-20 word avg sentences, no em dashes, subhead under 120 chars.
